local_names.chinese_simplified: 泰式炒米粉（加虾和豆芽）
local_names.chinese_traditional: 泰式炒米粉（加蝦和豆芽）

aliases: Thai fried rice noodles with shrimp
food_subgroup: Stir-fried rice noodles with shrimp, egg, and peanuts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Pad Thai. Nearest parent is a stir-fried rice noodle dish. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite noodle dishes — stir-fried rice noodles with seafood and egg (closest parent)

main_ingredients: rice vermicelli or flat rice noodles

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical plate of Pad Thai at a Thai restaurant or hawker stall in Singapore weighs approximately 350g and consists of rice noodles stir-fried with prawns, scrambled egg, bean sprouts, and fried tofu, seasoned with tamarind, fish sauce, and palm sugar, garnished with crushed peanuts, dried shrimp, lime wedges, and fresh spring onion.

nutrient_source.title: Pad Thai — FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; HPB Singapore Foodo Food Code F05060073
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match found in HPB Foodo under food code F05060073 — Pad Thai, standard preparation. Cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Thai food data.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — rice noodles, cooked; composite GI estimated. Tamarind and sugar addition may raise GI slightly.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 1673mg per serving (350g) — about 73% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by fish sauce, dried shrimp, and soy seasoning.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce in the Pad Thai preparation; the fish sauce is the primary sodium source and can be moderated without significantly altering the dish's flavour profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles have a low-moderate GI (~47); added palm sugar in the sauce contributes approximately 4.5g sugar per 100g; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 31 is moderate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients can consume Pad Thai in moderation; requesting reduced palm sugar in the preparation will lower the glycaemic load; the peanuts and shrimp add protein that helps moderate glucose absorption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Shrimp provides omega-3 fatty acids; tamarind has antioxidant properties; roasted peanuts add vitamin E and healthy fats.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pad Thai is an acceptable meal choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the shrimp provides omega-3 fatty acids, and the tamarind-based seasoning has antioxidant properties, making it a relatively anti-inflammatory hawker option.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pad Thai is Thailand's most internationally recognised noodle dish — thin rice noodles stir-fried in a screaming-hot wok with shrimp, scrambled egg, bean sprouts, and fried tofu, seasoned with a balance of tamarind paste (sour), fish sauce (umami-salty), and palm sugar (sweet), garnished with crushed roasted peanuts, dried shrimp, fresh spring onion, and a lime wedge. Originally promoted as a national dish by the Thai government in the 1930s, it is ubiquitous at Thai restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore.
common_user_queries: how many calories in pad thai
can a diabetic eat pad thai
pad thai vs char kway teow which is healthier
how much sodium in pad thai

confidence_reason: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo under food code F05060073 — Pad Thai; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Thai food data.
notes: Not halal in traditional Thai preparation due to dried shrimp paste (belacan) used at some stalls. Fish sauce (nam pla) is also not halal-certified by default at many non-certified establishments. Halal certified versions are available at halal Thai restaurants in Singapore. Peanut allergy is a critical concern.
